needs.name: Spring/well/river as drinking source
needs.notes: H=own well; N=shared well; W=ward tap; V=village piped

barriers.description: Not every household has a spring/well on their land; sharing may be necessary

feasibility.notes: Household wells and protected springs are feasible in some settlements but terrain varies.

evidence.citation: Rural Living in Nepal: Housing, Water & Waste in Paanchkhal
evidence.notes: Supports the importance and diversity of rural water sources and domestic water arrangements.
Household rainwater capture is a common supplementary water practice where storage vessels and roofs are available, though reliability is seasonal.
Gravity-fed and piped systems are standard collective rural water arrangements where source protection, distribution, and maintenance exceed single-household capacity.
Boiling, filtering, and basic household treatment are widely recognized household-level practices for safer drinking water.
Rural water systems routinely require storage at household or shared level because collection and use are rarely perfectly continuous.
Water user committees are inherently collective governance arrangements for shared infrastructure and cannot be meaningfully reduced to isolated households.
Traditional irrigation canals in Nepal are typically shared and maintained by multiple farming households rather than privately duplicated per family.
Irrigation pumps can be owned individually or shared, but effective use still depends on water source access, pipes, fuel, or electricity.
Pipes and hoses are modular irrigation inputs that can exist at household or shared scale depending on field layout and cost sharing.

needs.name: Power tiller, tractor for land prep

feasibility.notes: Household ownership is possible but expensive and maintenance-heavy; shared use is often more efficient.

evidence.notes: Household ownership is physically possible but may be economically inferior to shared access in most villages.
Emergency water points are usually planned as shared resilience infrastructure because their purpose is collective fallback access during failure or disaster.
Household firewood use remains a common cooking fuel pattern in rural Nepal where biomass access persists.
Household biogas is feasible where livestock numbers, water, and maintenance capacity are sufficient, but adoption varies by settlement conditions.
LPG access depends on road delivery, market supply, and recurring cash expenditure, making it a household consumption need with long external dependency.
Improved cookstoves are typically adopted and used at household level even when programs promoting them are organized collectively.
Rural electrification in Nepal commonly relies on household solar and community-scale hydro where grid extension is weak; exact deployment mix remains location-specific.
Grid connection and village-scale distribution are collective infrastructure systems that depend on external networks, regulation, and capital investment.
Street lighting serves shared public circulation space and is therefore a ward or village public good rather than a standalone household asset.

barriers.description: Electric/diesel mill too expensive per household; hand mill is H-feasible

evidence.notes: Mechanical milling can exist from household hand tools to village electric mills, but larger powered systems usually become shared productive infrastructure.
Cold storage generally requires reliable energy, equipment maintenance, and enough throughput to justify collective deployment.
Seed systems in rural agriculture commonly mix household seed saving with shared exchange or community banking for resilience and varietal access.
Compost, manure, and small-scale fertilizer use are typically managed at household or nearby shared scale in village agriculture.

feasibility.notes: Low-cost household ownership is realistic for basic tools.

evidence.notes: Basic agricultural hand tools are typically cheap and individually ownable relative to larger productive assets.
Household staple production is the core self-sufficiency case in subsistence-oriented rural farming systems.
Kitchen gardens are a common household-level mechanism for dietary diversity and short-cycle produce supply.
Smallholder livestock is commonly owned and managed at household level, even when grazing or veterinary services are partly shared.
Household grain storage is a standard component of food security in agrarian villages and directly supports self-reliance between harvests.
Produce cold storage usually needs shared infrastructure, stable energy, and sufficient market volume, making ward or village placement more realistic than household duplication.

needs.name: Rice mill, oil mill, flour mill

barriers.description: Electric mill expensive for single household; hand mill is H-feasible but slow
Electric mill needs electricity supply

feasibility.notes: Hand mills are feasible quickly; powered mills require more capital and energy.

evidence.citation: Post-harvest practices of horticultural crops in Nepal: Issues and management
evidence.notes: Supports the importance of local processing and storage capacity, though household-vs-shared deployment remains a planning judgment.
Weekly markets require enough buyers, sellers, and circulation to function, which makes village-scale aggregation the practical minimum.
Local retail shops serve a shared customer base and therefore usually emerge at ward or village scale rather than household scale.
School meals are tied to collective schooling provision and program administration, not independent household delivery inside the model.
Houses are household assets, but construction often relies on reciprocal labor and shared local material knowledge.
Everyday thermal comfort and household lighting are primarily managed within the home, even when fuel or electricity has external dependencies.
Basic sanitation policy and practice both favor household toilet access, even where waste treatment beyond the plot may require shared systems.
Clothing needs can be met through household production, local tailoring, or market purchase depending on skill, income, and access.
In remote Nepal, foot trails and bridges are foundational transport infrastructure and remain essential even where roads expand unevenly.
Pack transport may be individually owned or shared among nearby households where roads are absent and terrain constrains vehicles.
Motor roads are public infrastructure requiring collective construction, maintenance, and external state coordination.
Radio use can be individual household consumption even when the broader information ecosystem includes village announcements and community broadcasting.

needs.name: Mobile, telephony
needs.notes: Tower is V-level; handset is H

evidence.notes: Mobile use is a household-level consumption need, but functionality depends on externally provided network coverage and device affordability.

barriers.description: Needs tower/fiber and devices; H-level possible with mobile data
Device and data plan cost

feasibility.notes: Household access is feasible where coverage exists, but recurring cost remains high.

evidence.notes: Household internet feasibility depends heavily on signal availability, device ownership, and recurring data affordability.
Early childhood development centers depend on enough children, caregivers, and organized community or institutional support, which makes them collective services.

barriers.description: Needs enough children for classes; teacher must be paid
Trained teachers required

feasibility.notes: Ward-level schooling is feasible only where enrollment and staffing can be sustained.

evidence.citation: Access to infrastructure and human well-being: evidence from rural Nepal
evidence.notes: Supports the importance of local service access and the education burden in remote areas; ward-level placement remains a model judgment tied to population.
Vocational training usually depends on specialist instructors, equipment, and organized cohorts, which makes village or higher aggregation the realistic minimum in this model.

barriers.description: Needs trained health worker, medical supplies
Medicine stock, equipment require ongoing funding

feasibility.notes: Ward-level health services are feasible with recurrent staffing and drug supply.

evidence.citation: Assessing the health impacts of a Comprehensive Rural Health Project in Nepal
evidence.notes: Supports the importance of localized rural health service provision and community health interventions.
Hospital and referral care require specialist infrastructure, staffing, and regulation beyond ordinary household or ward capacity.
Maternal and child health spans household care and formal service access, making multi-scope placement appropriate in the model.
Formal ward and municipal administration is inherently collective and cannot be reduced to household or neighbor scope.
Civil documentation and land registration are formal state-recognized processes that inherently depend on collective administrative institutions.
Village disputes are often mediated first through nearby social structures before escalating to wider formal institutions, making multi-scope placement realistic.
Banking infrastructure depends on regulated financial systems, external capital, and institutional interfaces beyond neighborhood or household scope.
Savings groups and cooperatives depend on group trust, pooled capital, and collective governance rather than isolated household operation.
Parma is inherently a neighbor-scale reciprocal labor arrangement and cannot exist as a one-household activity.

barriers.description: Forest is a shared natural resource; cannot be divided per-household
Community forest regulations require collective management

evidence.notes: Community forest governance is inherently collective and not realistically reducible to isolated household ownership.
Grain and seed banks are collective resilience institutions that depend on shared governance, contribution rules, and managed reserves.
Household preservation by drying, pickling, and fermentation is a common short-cycle technique for off-season food security.
Rural waste handling typically mixes household composting and drainage with shared cleanup or disposal infrastructure when density rises.
Shared religious and community spaces serve collective ritual and gathering functions rather than isolated household use.
Festivals are collective cultural events whose meaning and viability depend on participation beyond the household.
Cottage craft production often happens inside the household while still benefiting from neighbor-scale sharing of tools, skill, or exchange.
Processing mills usually need enough throughput, power, and maintenance to justify shared deployment rather than strict household ownership.
Roads, bridges, and public lighting are classic shared infrastructure systems requiring collective planning and maintenance.
Early warning and organized disaster response require shared communication and collective coordination beyond single households.
Family rights practices may be experienced at household level, but legal recognition and enforceability depend on wider formal institutions.

evidence.citation: Post-harvest practices of horticultural crops in Nepal: Issues and management
evidence.notes: Supports the importance of local processing and storage capacity, though household-vs-shared deployment remains a planning judgment.
